meta.note: Low Countries + Switzerland/Nordics + Bulgaria + Baltic states sweep. Ban-adds: Fanny Hill (DK), Lady Chatterley's Lover (LV). Held: Lithuania's Auksa altorius (a Latin-script/language press ban, not a title-content ban). No Switzerland or Iceland title-specific bans surfaced. Pavlov's three collections use transliterated Bulgarian titles. These are mostly liberal jurisdictions with thin formal-ban records (obscenity/blasphemy/war-era), except Bulgaria (communist-era).

entries.title: Een Bloemhof van allerley lieflijkheyd
entries.description_ban: Radical-Enlightenment encyclopaedic dictionary suppressed in Amsterdam for 'blasphemous remarks about God and Jesus Christ'; copies confiscated and the author fled.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific 17th-c. religious ban; documented via Wikipedia and Historiek.

entries.title: Een Ligt schijnende in duystere plaatsen
entries.description_ban: Koerbagh's most radical (Spinozist) work; betrayed by his printer, it triggered his arrest and 27 July 1668 blasphemy conviction (ten years' hard labour; he died in 1669).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific religious ban and conviction; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Gangreen 1: Black Venus
entries.description_ban: A month after winning the Triennial State Prize for Prose, the novel was seized from a Brussels bookshop on the Justice Minister's order as 'morally degenerate'; released roughly a week later.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific government seizure; documented via Wikipedia and Flanders Literature.

entries.title: Juhannustanssit
entries.description_ban: Helsinki City Court convicted Salama of blasphemy (1966) over a sermon-parody scene; censored editions circulated until the uncut text was finally published in the 1990s. Pardoned by President Kekkonen in 1968.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific blasphemy conviction; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Uten en tråd
entries.description_ban: Bjørneboe and publisher Pax were convicted over the erotic novel 'Without a Stitch'; copies were confiscated and the book banned. Reissued 1988; the ban was never formally lifted.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific obscenity conviction and confiscation; documented by Skeivt Arkiv.

entries.title: Albertine
entries.description_ban: Seized by police the day after publication on the Justice Minister's order over a rape scene; the Supreme Court of Norway upheld the confiscation in 1888 and fined Krohg.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific seizure upheld by the Supreme Court; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Nazisthelvetet
entries.description_ban: Anti-Nazi book; a Swedish court convicted Holmgren for breaching wartime neutrality and the book was confiscated. He reissued the identical text as 'Nazistparadiset' (1943).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific wartime confiscation; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Samtal med Hitler
entries.description_ban: The Swedish wartime government, under German pressure, suppressed the 1940 Swedish edition of this anti-Nazi book, reportedly confiscated soon after it left the press.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific wartime suppression; documented via Wikipedia (medium confidence).

entries.title: Fascism
entries.description_ban: 10,000 copies of Zhelev's study of fascist totalitarianism (Фашизмът) were pulled from bookstores and libraries about three weeks after release because its critique read as a critique of communism. A landmark dissident case.

entries.title: Litse
entries.description_ban: Novel (Лице / Face) criticising communist rule; the climactic chapter was cut by censors and on release day the print run was vanned off to a repository. Restored after 1989.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by The File Room (NCAC).

entries.title: The Roof
entries.description_ban: Novel (Покривът) allegorising the 1959 collapse of the Lenin steel-mill roof in Pernik; halted mid-printing and banned from publication, not released until after communism (2007).

entries.title: Satiri
entries.description_ban: Pavlov's debut collection ('Satires') was confiscated by the authorities before it reached readers, beginning a decade-plus publishing ban.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation; documented by the Global Literature in Libraries Initiative.

entries.title: Stihove
entries.description_ban: Pavlov's second collection ('Poems'/Verses) was also confiscated before public distribution, followed by a roughly ten-year publishing ban from 1966.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific confiscation; documented by the Global Literature in Libraries Initiative.

entries.title: Stari neshta
entries.description_ban: Pavlov's third volume ('Old Things'), published on his 50th birthday, triggered renewed suppression of his work lasting until 1989.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific re-suppression; documented by the Global Literature in Libraries Initiative (medium confidence).

entries.title: Gintarinė širdis
entries.description_ban: Children's fairy-tale collection ('Amber Heart') whose two same-sex-themed stories were ruled to breach the minors-protection law; distribution was suspended, then re-released with an 'unsuitable under 14' label. The ECtHR found a free-expression violation (Macatė v. Lithuania, 2023).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific restriction, later ruled unlawful by the ECtHR; documented by ILGA-Europe.

entries.title: Untitled 12
entries.description_ban: Kender was prosecuted for child pornography over this prose work depicting child sexual abuse; Harju County Court acquitted him in 2016 (ruled literary, not pornographic), upheld on appeal in 2017.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific obscenity prosecution (acquitted); documented by ERR (medium confidence, borderline book).

entries.title: Fanny Hill
entries.description_ban: The Danish publisher of an unexpurgated edition was prosecuted in 1963; the case ended in a 1964 acquittal, a key step toward Denmark's 1967 decriminalisation of literary pornography. Adds a Denmark case to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Denmark obscenity prosecution; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Lady Chatterley's Lover
entries.description_ban: Under Kārlis Ulmanis's authoritarian regime, Latvian post-censorship banned the sale of the novel on moral grounds. Adds a Latvia ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Latvia ban; documented via Wikipedia citing translation-studies scholarship (medium confidence).
